edges.description: Disrupted lineage programming reduces or distorts the interneuron progenitor pool produced in the ganglionic eminences.
edges.source: ARX Loss of Function and Subpallial Patterning Disruption
edges.target: Interneuron Progenitor Specification and Differentiation Failure

edges.description: Disrupted lineage programming also impairs the migratory competence of interneuron precursors leaving the subpallium.
edges.source: ARX Loss of Function and Subpallial Patterning Disruption
edges.target: Tangential Migration Failure from Ganglionic Eminences

edges.description: ARX loss disrupts basal ganglia differentiation and the thalamocortical axon tracts that traverse the basal ganglia, an ARX-specific branch.
edges.source: ARX Loss of Function and Subpallial Patterning Disruption
edges.target: Impaired Basal Ganglia Differentiation and Thalamocortical Wiring

edges.description: ARX is also required outside the cortex for genital/testicular development, producing the abnormal genitalia of XLAG in males.
edges.source: ARX Loss of Function and Subpallial Patterning Disruption

edges.description: Reduced interneuron generation lowers the number of inhibitory interneurons available to populate developing cortical circuits.
edges.source: Interneuron Progenitor Specification and Differentiation Failure
edges.target: Cortical GABAergic Interneuron Deficit and Mislocalization

edges.description: Migration failure produces regionally depleted or misplaced cortical inhibitory interneurons even when some progenitors are generated.
edges.source: Tangential Migration Failure from Ganglionic Eminences
edges.target: Cortical GABAergic Interneuron Deficit and Mislocalization

edges.description: Defective ARX-dependent neuronal migration contributes to the agyria-pachygyria lissencephaly pattern of XLAG.
edges.source: Tangential Migration Failure from Ganglionic Eminences

edges.description: Deficient inhibitory interneuron integration reduces inhibitory tone and destabilizes cortical network activity.
edges.source: Cortical GABAergic Interneuron Deficit and Mislocalization

edges.description: Cortical inhibitory circuit failure impairs neurodevelopmental function across the ARX spectrum.
edges.source: Cortical GABAergic Interneuron Deficit and Mislocalization

edges.description: Early-life excitation-inhibition imbalance manifests as intractable neonatal seizures in XLAG.
Milder ARX-spectrum inhibitory circuit dysfunction can manifest as infantile spasms or West syndrome.
Impaired basal ganglia differentiation produces the dysplastic basal ganglia imaging phenotype.
edges.source: Impaired Basal Ganglia Differentiation and Thalamocortical Wiring

edges.description: ARX-dependent genital and testicular developmental disruption produces ambiguous or hypoplastic genitalia in XLAG.
Forebrain proliferative and patterning defects contribute to microcephaly in severe ARX loss-of-function disease.
ARX-related forebrain maldevelopment contributes to corpus callosum agenesis.

edges.target: ARX Loss of Function and Subpallial Patterning Disruption

evidence.reference_title: Loss-of-function ARX mutations cause XLAG.
evidence.snippet: We found multiple loss-of-function mutations in ARX in individuals affected with XLAG and in some female relatives, and conclude that mutation of ARX causes XLAG.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations of ARX are associated with striking pleiotropy.
evidence.snippet: Together, the group of phenotypes associated with ARX mutations demonstrates remarkable pleiotropy, but also comprises a nearly continuous series of developmental disorders that begins with hydranencephaly, lissencephaly, and agenesis of the corpus callosum, and ends with a series of overlapping syndromes with apparently normal brain structure.

nodes.description: Loss-of-function mutations in ARX disrupt the paired-like homeodomain transcriptional-repressor programs that pattern the ventral telencephalon and specify cortical GABAergic interneuron lineages from ganglionic eminence-derived progenitor domains. This is the initiating molecular lesion of the ARX interneuronopathy and of XLAG at the severe end of the spectrum.
nodes.id: ARX Loss of Function and Subpallial Patterning Disruption

evidence.reference_title: Inactivation of Arx leads to severe disorganization of the ventral telencephalon with impaired neuronal migration and differentiation.
evidence.snippet: ARX loss-of-function mutations cause X-linked lissencephaly with ambiguous genitalia (XLAG), a severe neurological condition that results in profound brain malformations, including microcephaly, absence of corpus callosum, and impairment of the basal ganglia.

evidence.reference_title: Conditional inactivation of Arx in developing interneurons.
evidence.snippet: Aristaless-related homeobox (ARX) is a paired-like homeodomain transcription factor that functions primarily as a transcriptional repressor and has been implicated in neocortical interneuron specification and migration.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation of ARX causes abnormal development of forebrain and testes in mice and XLAG in humans.
evidence.snippet: Male embryonic mice with mutations in the X-linked aristaless-related homeobox gene (Arx) developed with small brains due to suppressed proliferation and regional deficiencies in the forebrain.

nodes.description: ARX is required outside the cortex for genital and testicular development, so loss of function produces the ambiguous or hypoplastic genitalia in hemizygous males that gives XLAG its name. This branch is ARX-specific and independent of the cortical interneuron skeleton.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation of ARX causes abnormal development of forebrain and testes in mice.
evidence.snippet: These mice also showed aberrant migration and differentiation of interneurons containing gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABAergic interneurons) in the ganglionic eminence and neocortex as well as abnormal testicular differentiation.

evidence.reference_title: Loss-of-function ARX mutations cause XLAG in humans.
evidence.snippet: We found multiple loss-of-function mutations in ARX in individuals affected with XLAG and in some female relatives, and conclude that mutation of ARX causes XLAG.

nodes.description: Agenesis of the corpus callosum is a consistent commissural abnormality in XLAG and in the related Proud syndrome end of the ARX spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: XLAG MRI: agenesis of the corpus callosum.
evidence.snippet: Brain magnetic resonance imaging shows anterior pachygyria and posterior agyria with a mildly thick cortex, agenesis of the corpus callosum, and dysplastic basal ganglia.

nodes.description: Ambiguous or hypoplastic genitalia in hemizygous males is the defining extracerebral feature that gives X-linked lissencephaly with abnormal genitalia its name.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation of ARX causes XLAG with abnormal genitalia in humans.
evidence.snippet: These characteristics recapitulate some of the clinical features of X-linked lissencephaly with abnormal genitalia (XLAG) in humans.

nodes.description: The developing ARX-mutant neocortex contains too few GABAergic interneurons, or interneurons arrested in the white matter and subventricular zone, impairing inhibitory circuit assembly. In severe human XLAG the cortical plate can be almost devoid of interneurons.
nodes.id: Cortical GABAergic Interneuron Deficit and Mislocalization

evidence.reference_title: Mutant ARX: reduced GAD/calretinin neocortical interneurons.
evidence.snippet: We found that glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD)- and calretinin (CR)-containing cells were significantly reduced in the neocortex and located in the white matter and neocortical subventricular zone, while neuropeptide Y- or cholecystokinin-containing cells were normally distributed.

evidence.reference_title: Tangential migration impairment in ARX lissencephaly.
evidence.snippet: In the ARX-mutated brain, the cortical plate contained almost exclusively pyramidal cells and was devoid of interneurons.

evidence.reference_title: Conditional Arx loss: reduced interneurons of all subtypes.
evidence.snippet: The result of this developmental shift is a reduced number of interneurons (all subtypes) at early postnatal and later time periods.

nodes.description: Dysplastic basal ganglia, reflecting impaired ARX-dependent basal ganglia differentiation, are a characteristic imaging feature of XLAG.

evidence.reference_title: XLAG MRI: dysplastic basal ganglia.
evidence.snippet: Brain magnetic resonance imaging shows anterior pachygyria and posterior agyria with a mildly thick cortex, agenesis of the corpus callosum, and dysplastic basal ganglia.

nodes.description: Cortical inhibitory circuit failure shifts excitation-inhibition balance and drives early-life seizures, ranging from the intractable neonatal seizures of XLAG to the infantile spasms/West syndrome of polyalanine-expansion ARX disease.

evidence.reference_title: Targeted Arx deletion from ganglionic eminence-derived neurons causes developmental epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: Arx(-/y);Dlx5/6(CIG) (male) mice exhibit a variety of seizure types beginning in early-life, including seizures that behaviourally and electroencephalographically resembles infantile spasms, and show evolution through development.

evidence.reference_title: XLAG: intractable seizures from the first day of life.
evidence.snippet: Male patients with X-linked lissencephaly with abnormal genitalia show intractable seizures, especially clonic convulsions or myoclonus from the first day of life, but neither infantile spasms nor hypsarrhythmia on electroencephalograms so far.

nodes.description: Beyond the cortical interneuron branch, ARX loss impairs basal ganglia differentiation and the formation of thalamocortical axon tracts that must traverse the basal ganglia, contributing to the dysmorphic basal ganglia and diencephalic/thalamocortical abnormalities characteristic of XLAG. This is an ARX-specific branch not part of the shared interneuron module skeleton.
nodes.id: Impaired Basal Ganglia Differentiation and Thalamocortical Wiring

evidence.reference_title: Inactivation of Arx: loss of cholinergic neurons and impaired thalamocortical projections.
evidence.snippet: Furthermore, Arx mutants lacked a large fraction of cholinergic neurons and displayed a strong impairment of thalamocortical projections, in which major axon fiber tracts failed to traverse the basal ganglia.

nodes.description: Polyalanine-expansion ARX mutations cause X-linked infantile spasms/West syndrome at the milder end of the spectrum, distinct from the agyria of XLAG.

evidence.reference_title: ARX polyalanine expansion causes West syndrome.
evidence.snippet: polyalanine expansion of ARX causes symptomatic or nonsymptomatic West's syndrome and nonsyndromic mental retardation

nodes.description: Nonsyndromic intellectual disability, with structurally normal brains, is the mildest recurrent ARX presentation and part of the ARX pleiotropic spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: ARX polyalanine expansion causes nonsyndromic mental retardation.
evidence.snippet: polyalanine expansion of ARX causes symptomatic or nonsymptomatic West's syndrome and nonsyndromic mental retardation

nodes.description: ARX-deficient interneuron progenitors fail to execute normal GABAergic differentiation programs after early patterning, leaving immature neurons that accumulate in the ganglionic eminences instead of producing mature cortical inhibitory interneurons.
nodes.id: Interneuron Progenitor Specification and Differentiation Failure

evidence.reference_title: Inactivation of Arx leads to impaired neuronal migration and differentiation.
evidence.snippet: In these animals, the early differentiation of this tissue appeared normal, whereas subsequent differentiation was impaired, leading to the periventricular accumulation of immature neurons in both the lateral ganglionic eminence and medial ganglionic eminence (MGE).

evidence.reference_title: Tangential migration impairment in LIS1, DCX, and ARX lissencephaly.
evidence.snippet: The ganglionic eminences and basal ganglia were poorly cellular, suggesting an interneuron production and/or differentiation defect.

nodes.description: Intractable seizures, typically clonic convulsions or myoclonus from the first day of life, are a hallmark of XLAG and reflect the cortical interneuron deficit.

evidence.reference_title: XLAG: intractable seizures from the first day of life.
evidence.snippet: Male patients with X-linked lissencephaly with abnormal genitalia show intractable seizures, especially clonic convulsions or myoclonus from the first day of life, but neither infantile spasms nor hypsarrhythmia on electroencephalograms so far.

nodes.description: Anterior pachygyria with posterior agyria and a mildly thickened cortex is the core cortical malformation of XLAG, at the severe end of the ARX spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: XLAG MRI: anterior pachygyria and posterior agyria.
evidence.snippet: Brain magnetic resonance imaging shows anterior pachygyria and posterior agyria with a mildly thick cortex, agenesis of the corpus callosum, and dysplastic basal ganglia.

nodes.description: Microcephaly is part of the severe XLAG brain-malformation phenotype and is also noted in severe ARX genotype-phenotype series.

evidence.reference_title: ARX mutations and genotype-phenotype correlation.
evidence.snippet: A nonconservative missense mutation near the C-terminal aristaless domain caused unusually severe XLAG with microcephaly and mild cerebellar hypoplasia.

evidence.reference_title: ARX loss of function causes XLAG with microcephaly.
evidence.snippet: ARX loss-of-function mutations cause X-linked lissencephaly with ambiguous genitalia (XLAG), a severe neurological condition that results in profound brain malformations, including microcephaly, absence of corpus callosum, and impairment of the basal ganglia.

nodes.description: ARX-deficient interneuron precursors fail to migrate tangentially from the medial and lateral ganglionic eminences into the developing neocortex. XLAG is the prototypical human disorder demonstrating deficient tangential migration in the brain.
nodes.id: Tangential Migration Failure from Ganglionic Eminences

evidence.reference_title: Inactivation of Arx: greatly reduced tangential and radial migration.
evidence.snippet: Both tangential migration toward the cortex and striatum and radial migration to the globus pallidus and striatum were greatly reduced in the mutants, causing a periventricular accumulation of NPY+ or calretinin+ neurons in the MGE.

evidence.reference_title: Mutant ARX: abnormal distribution of GABAergic interneurons in human neocortex.
evidence.snippet: Our findings suggest that ARX protein controls not only the tangential migration of GABAergic interneurons from the ganglionic eminence, but also may serve to induce radial migration from the neocortical subventricular zone.

evidence.reference_title: XLAG as a tangential migration disorder: proposal for interneuronopathy.
evidence.snippet: X-linked lissencephaly with abnormal genitalia is the first human disorder in which deficient tangential migration in the brain has been demonstrated.
